items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.

items.correct: The ability of different classes to respond to the same method call in their own way.
items.misconception: Having multiple classes with the same name.
items.random_wrong: Variables that change type at runtime.
items.why: Students confuse polymorphism with naming. Polymorphism means 'many forms' - a single interface (method signature) behaving differently depending on the object's actual class.

items.q: What is the purpose of database normalisation?
items.correct: To reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies.
items.misconception: To make the database run faster.
items.random_wrong: To make the database look neater.
items.why: Students think normalisation is about performance. In fact, heavy normalisation often slows queries. Its purpose is DATA INTEGRITY - preventing inconsistencies by storing each fact once.

items.q: Which gate outputs 1 only when both inputs are 1?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: AND. “OR” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does a NOT gate do to its input?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Inverts it. “Doubles it” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many rows are in the truth table for a 3-input AND gate?
items.why: The real figure is 8 rows. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
